summary:
Gustavus Adolphus of Sweden (r. 1611–1632 AD) was the most innovative military commander of the early 17th century, the 'Lion of the North' who transformed Sweden from a peripheral Baltic state into a major European power during the [[event:2328|Thirty Years' War]] through tactical and organizational military reforms that established the model for early modern European warfare. His military revolution combined several innovations: smaller, more flexible infantry units (brigades of 500–600 rather than the massive Spanish tercios of 3,000+) capable of firepower and maneuver; mobile field artillery (lightweight 3-pound 'regimental guns' assigned to infantry units) that gave infantry their own organic artillery support; an expansion of cavalry from passive to aggressive shock action; and combined-arms coordination that integrated all three arms into a mutually supporting tactical system.

Gustavus first tested these innovations in wars against Denmark, Russia, and Poland, transforming Sweden into a Baltic power and training his army before entering the Thirty Years' War in 1630. His intervention saved the German Protestant cause: the [[event:2404|Battle of Breitenfeld]] (1631) was the first major Protestant victory of the war; the subsequent rapid campaign across southern Germany demonstrated the unprecedented strategic mobility of his army. He died at the Battle of Lützen (November 1632), shot while leading a cavalry charge in thick fog — a death that made him a Protestant martyr but deprived Sweden of its most capable commander just as his campaigns were reaching maximum effect. His chancellor Axel Oxenstierna continued his policies, and Sweden remained a major European power (receiving territories in the [[event:2329|Peace of Westphalia]]) for decades after his death. Napoleon later cited Gustavus Adolphus alongside Alexander and Caesar as one of the seven great captains of history.
